text: The surgeon uses a hysteroscope to inspect the uterine cavity, visualizing bilateral osteo and confirming the absence of septum or cavity abnormalities. Upon retracting the hysteroscope to the lower uterine segment, a concave defect is observed on the anterior wall, indicating a cesarean scar niche. A hysteroscopic resectoscope is employed to denude the defect, followed by the removal of the hysteroscope and insertion of a uterine manipulator.

The surgeon performs diagnostic laparoscopy, visually confirming the uteroperitoneal fistulas tract and dense omental adhesions. Cold laparoscopic scissors are used to carefully remove the adhesions, avoiding the bladder due to the presence of severe adhesions in the vesicovaginal and vesicocervical planes.

The surgeon begins dissection laterally near the obliterated umbilical vessels using a CO-II laser to make an incision on the peritoneum, entering new spaces on both sides. The dissection is extended through the vesicle cervical space, combining the bilateral spaces. The CO-II laser and blunt dissection are used to mobilize the bladder, allowing precise and superficial dissection to minimize thermal spread and injury. Dissection is performed inferiorly to create adequate space for the excision and repair of the uterine niche.

The surgeon carefully dissects dense fibrotic tissue layer by layer to identify and map the fistula tract, reaching the defect at the anterior uterine wall. The uterine manipulator is removed, and the hysteroscope is introduced transvaginally to the lower uterine segment, advancing into the niche under direct laparoscopic visualization. The thinned myometrium is visible due to the extrusion of the hysteroscope and the pressure from saline infusion, facilitating laparoscopic mapping and excision of the defect.

The surgeon performs a wide laparoscopic excision of the peripheral tissue surrounding the uterine niche, allowing the hysteroscope to penetrate into the abdominal cavity. The fistula is excised and sent for pathological examination. The defect is repaired in two layers using zero V-lock suture, with fresh bleeding at the edges controlled and hemostasis achieved. The repair is confirmed to be intact through both laparoscopic and hysteroscopic visualization.
